coredata.dc:title: Label-free upconversion nanoparticles-based fluorescent probes for sequential sensing of Cu2+, pyrophosphate and alkaline phosphatase activity
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Fangfang
Zhang, Cuiling
Xue, Qin
Li, Huaping
Xian, Yuezhong
coredata.dc:description: An efficient near-infrared fluorescence probe has been developed for the sequential detection of Cu2+, pyrophosphate (P2O7 4−, PPi), and alkaline phosphatase (ALP), which is based on the "off−on−off" fluorescence switch of branched polyethyleneimine (PEI)-capped NaGdF4:Yb/Tm upconversion nanoparticles (UCNPs). The fluorescence is quenched via energy transfer from UCNPs to Cu2+ for the coordination of PEI with Cu2+. The strong affinity between Cu2+ and PPi leads to the formation of Cu2+-PPi complex and results in the detachment of Cu2+ from the surface of UCNPs, thus the fluorescence is triggered on. ALP-directed hydrolysis of PPi causes the disassembly of Cu2+-PPi complex and re-conjugation between Cu2+ with PEI, which leads to the switch-off fluorescence of UCNPs. The system allows sequential analysis of Cu2+, PPi, and ALP by modulating the switch of the fluorescence of UCNPs with detection limits of 57.8nM, 184nM, and 0.019U/mL for Cu2+, PPi, and ALP, respectively. By virtue of the NIR feature and excellent biocompatibility, the UCNPs-based probes are suitable for bioimaging. Taking Cu2+ visualization as a model, the nanoprobes have been successfully applied for intracellular imaging of Cu2+ in living cells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Upconversion nanoparticles
Fluorescent probe
Copper ions
Pyrophosphate ions
Alkaline phosphatase
Bioimaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence responses towards Cu2+, PPi and ALP
Detection of PPi in human urine
Detection of ALP in serum samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Fluorescence spectra of UCNPs with various concentration of Cu2+(0, 0.1, 0.2, 0.3, 0.6, 0.9, 1.3, 1.5, 1.8, 2.0, and 3μM) (λex =980nm). (B) The fluorescence quenching efficiency (1－F/F0, monitored at 796nm) against Cu2+ concentration (0.1−3.0μM). F0 and F represent the emission intensity of the probe before and after adding Cu2+, respectively. The inset in Fig. 1B is the linear plot for Cu2+ detection. (C) Selectivity of the UCNPs-based probe (0.1mg/mL) for Cu2+(2.0μM) over other metal ions (20.0μM). (D) The plausible energy transfer mechanism from UCNPs to Cu2+.

(A) Fluorescence spectra towards PPi with different concentrations (0, 0.5, 2, 3, 4, 6, 8, and 10μM). (B) Plot of the change in fluorescence ratio versus concentration of PPi, where F0 and F represent the fluorescence intensity of the probe before and after adding PPi, respectively. Inset is the linear plot from 0.5 to 8.0μM. (C) Selectivity for PPi detection. The concentration for the tested substances is 8.0μM. F and F0 are the fluorescence intensity in the presence and absence of analytes, respectively.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of UCNPs in the presence of ALP with different concentration (0, 0.0625, 0.125, 0.25, 0.375, 0.5, 0.625, 0.75, 0.875 and 1U/mL). (B) The plot of the fluorescence quenching efficiency versus the concentration of ALP over the range from 0.0625 to 1.0U/mL. F and F0 are the fluorescence intensities in the presence and absence of ALP, respectively. Inset is the corresponding linear curve (0.0625–0.875U/mL). (C) Selectivity for ALP detection. The concentration is 1U/mL for ALP, 10U/mL for EXO I, GOD and 0.35mg/mL for lysozyme, BSA and trypsin. F and F0 are the fluorescence intensities in the presence and absence of different analytes, respectively.

(A) Fluorescence spectra in the presence of different concentration of ALP (0, 0.125, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1, 1.25, 1.5, and 1.75U/mL) in diluted bovine serum. (B) The plot of the fluorescence quenching efficiency versus the concentration of ALP over the range from 0.125 to 1.75U/mL, where F and F0 are the fluorescence intensity in the presence and absence of ALP, respectively. Inset is the corresponding linear curve (0.125–1.5U/mL).

Upconversion fluorescence images of HeLa cells incubated with (A) 100μg/mL PEI-capped UCNPs for 6h, and then successive treatment with exogenous Cu2+ source of (B) 140μM for 15min and (C) 80μM for 10min. Emission was collected from 450 to 500nm, λex =980nm. From left to right: bright-field channel, fluorescence channel, and the overlay imaging. Scale bar =20µm.
Schematic illustration of the fluorescence "off–on–off" switch for sequential detection of Cu2+, PPi and ALP with the PEI-capped UCNPs.

head.ce:title: Label-free upconversion nanoparticles-based fluorescent probes for sequential sensing of Cu2+, pyrophosphate and alkaline phosphatase activity

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shanghai Key Laboratory of Green Chemistry and Chemical Processes, Department of Chemistry, School of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, East China Normal University, Shanghai 200241, People's Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shanghai Key Laboratory of Green Chemistry and Chemical Processes, Department of Chemistry, School of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, East China Normal University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An efficient near-infrared fluorescence probe has been developed for the sequential detection of Cu2+, pyrophosphate (P2O7 4−, PPi), and alkaline phosphatase (ALP), which is based on the "off−on−off" fluorescence switch of branched polyethyleneimine (PEI)-capped NaGdF4:Yb/Tm upconversion nanoparticles (UCNPs). The fluorescence is quenched via energy transfer from UCNPs to Cu2+ for the coordination of PEI with Cu2+. The strong affinity between Cu2+ and PPi leads to the formation of Cu2+-PPi complex and results in the detachment of Cu2+ from the surface of UCNPs, thus the fluorescence is triggered on. ALP-directed hydrolysis of PPi causes the disassembly of Cu2+-PPi complex and re-conjugation between Cu2+ with PEI, which leads to the switch-off fluorescence of UCNPs. The system allows sequential analysis of Cu2+, PPi, and ALP by modulating the switch of the fluorescence of UCNPs with detection limits of 57.8nM, 184nM, and 0.019U/mL for Cu2+, PPi, and ALP, respectively. By virtue of the NIR feature and excellent biocompatibility, the UCNPs-based probes are suitable for bioimaging. Taking Cu2+ visualization as a model, the nanoprobes have been successfully applied for intracellular imaging of Cu2+ in living cells.

ce:section.ce:para: The highly selective and sensitive sensing of biologically important elements, such as Cu2+, pyrophosphate ion (P2O7 4−, PPi), and alkaline phosphatase (ALP), are very important to understand the physiological and pathological processes occurring in living systems. Cu2+ is an essential trace element for human beings and animals, and excessive Cu2+ can induce oxidative stress and result in neurodegenerative diseases, including Alzheimer's, Parkinson's and Wilson's diseases (Donnelly et al., 2007; Strausak et al., 2001; Valentine and Hart, 2003). PPi is a critical metabolite in various biochemical processes. And, many researches reveal that PPi can be used as indicators in some diseases, such as arthritis and chondrocalcinosis (Abhishek and Doherty, 2011). As for ALP, an essential hydrolase enzyme in phosphate metabolism, is responsible for the dephosphorylation process of proteins, nucleic acids, or small molecules in biological organisms (Coleman, 1992). In addition, abnormal level of ALP might be related to some diseases, such as dynamic bone disease, liver dysfunction, diabetes, breast and prostatic cancer (Ooi et al., 2007).

Owing to their biological importance, it is crucial to develop simple, facile and reliable methods for the determination of Cu2+, PPi and ALP. Among various strategies, fluorescent sensing technique has attracted much attention because of its simplicity, real-time and in vivo detection. Many fluorescent sensors were developed for the selective detection of Cu2+. Most of them were designed by covalent binding fluorescent organic moieties towards Cu2+. Based on a specific Cu2+-induced reversible ring-opening mechanism, rhodamine derivatives were widely used as organic fluorescence probes for Cu2+ detection in aqueous solution or biological system (Kar et al., 2013; Yu et al., 2008; Zhao et al., 2009). Recently, nanostructured materials were also used as probes for Cu2+ determination and bioimaging (Huang et al., 2015; Jin and Han, 2014; Liu et al., 2014; Qing et al., 2014; Qu et al., 2012; Sarkar et al., 2015; Tian et al., 2013; Tian et al., 2013; Yuan et al., 2014; Zou et al., 2016). As for PPi recognition, the general strategy is utilization of metal ion complexes as binding sites. Owing to the strong interaction of PPi with metal complexes, the fluorescent signal of the sensing system was changed and the determination of PPi was realized (Zhu et al., 2012; Niu et al., 2016). In addition, various nanomaterials were used to develop fluorescent sensing platform for ALP measurement based on the ability to transform PPi into orthophosphate and induce the change of fluorescence (Chen et al., 2014; Ma et al., 2016).

Although various nanostructured materials are used to construct fluorescent probes for Cu2+, PPi and ALP, most of them were excited by UV/visible light. It is well-known that near-infrared (NIR) light is more suitable for bioapplications because it causes less photodamage towards biosamples and a deeper penetration depth (Tsang et al., 2016; Wang et al., 2010; Wei et al., 2016). Upconversion nanoparticles (UCNPs) are luminescent nanomaterials that convert a NIR excitation into various upconversion emissions ranging from UV to NIR through lanthanide doping. Some UCNPs-based sensors were reported for the detection of Cu2+. For example, Zhang et al. reported a UCNPs-based probe for Cu2+ detection, in which the Cu2+-sensitive fluorescent dye (rhodamine B hydrazide) was conjugated onto mesoporous silica@β-NaYF4 nanocrystals (Li et al., 2012). Liu et al. developed a new strategy to construct upconversion probe by Cu2+-induced fluorescence quenching of UCNPs and further used for biothiol imaging (Liang et al., 2016). To the best of our knowledge, UCNPs-based probes for selective detection of PPi and ALP have not been reported to date.

The separated sensing strategies for multiple analytes often need tedious sampling, time-consuming pretreatments, and result in the low accuracy. Sequential detection with a single sensor has some advantages, such as potential low cost and more efficient analysis (Kang et al., 2017; Tian et al., 2013; Tian et al., 2013; Ren et al., 2016; Wang et al., 2014). Given the importance of Cu2+, PPi and ALP in biological system, the determination of these substances using UCNPs-based probes is very meaningful. Herein, we adopted a direct one-step solvothermal route to prepare NaGdF4:Yb/Tm UCNPs with branched polyethyleneimine (PEI) as the capping agent. And then, the PEI-capped UCNPs were used as probes for sequential determination of Cu2+, PPi and ALP based on "off–on–off" fluorescent switch. The framework of the proposed method for Cu2+, PPi, and ALP detection is illustrated in Scheme 1. The amino groups of PEI-capped UCNPs can coordinate with Cu2+, which leads to a strong quenching of the upconversion luminescence. In the presence of PPi, it can bind strongly with Cu2+ to form Cu2+-PPi complex (lgKCu-PPi=12.45) (English et al., 1997), and thus trigger on the fluorescence of UCNPs. Through the ALP directed enzymatic reaction, the fluorescence signal is further quenched because of the hydrolysis of PPi and successive release of Cu2+. Based on the fluorescence "off–on–off" status, a rapid and effective assay for label-free detection of Cu2+, PPi and ALP are developed, respectively. Taking the advantages of UCNPs, the nanoprobes have been applied for intracellular imaging of Cu2+ in HeLa cells as a proof-of-concept application.

Details on Material and methods including chemicals and materials, apparatus, synthesis of PEI-capped NaGdF4:Yb/Tm UCNPs, fluorescent assay procedures, cell cytotoxicity and bioimaging can be found in the Supplementary Information.

A typical TEM image of UCNPs shows nearly spherical and monodisperse (Fig. S1A). Inset in Fig. S1A exhibits the size distribution of the UCNPs, and the average diameter is around 48.9nm based on a detailed analysis of the particle size over 200 NPs. In addition, the result of dynamic light scattering indicates the average hydrodynamic radius of PEI-capped UCNPs is about 108.8nm (Fig. S2). The narrow size-distribution reveals no obvious agglomeration of the NPs. The XRD pattern of the UCNPs exhibits well agreement with the standard cubic pattern of NaGdF4, and no peaks from other phases or impurities are observed (Fig. S1B). Clear lattice fringes in the HRTEM image reveal high crystallinity of the UCNPs (Fig. S1C). The lattice spacing is about 0.32nm, which is consistent with the (111) plane of simple cubic structure of NaGdF4. The corresponding selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern (inset of Fig. S1C) indicates the single crystalline in nature. Furthermore, the elemental composition of the UCNPs is determined by EDX. As shown in Fig. S3, all the elements can be observed in the spectrum, including Na, Gd, F, Yb and Tm.

Branched PEI molecules were used as the capping agent to control the growth and enhance the water dispersity of the UCNPs. Zeta potential of the as-prepared UCNPs in colloidal form is about +33.5mV (Fig. S4). The positive value is ascribed to -NH2 on the surface of UCNPs, which guarantees the good stability of the colloid. The existence of amino groups is further verified by FT-IR spectroscopy (Fig. S5). The bands at 3438 and 1630cm−1 are related to the N－H stretching and bending vibration, respectively. Meanwhile, the absorption bands at 2958 and 2846cm−1 are attributed to the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of methylene (-CH2-), respectively. A sharp absorption band at 1385cm−1 is due to the stretching vibrations of C-N bonds, thereby revealing the successful conjugation of PEI on the surface of the NPs (Padhye et al., 2015; Wu et al., 2016).

The upconversion fluorescent spectrum of UCNPs under the excitation of 980nm is shown in Fig. S1D. The emission peaks of Tm3+ at 451, 478, 650, 702 and 796nm can be assigned to the 1D2→3F4, 1G4→3H6, 1G4→3F4, 3F2,3→3H6, 3H4→3H6 transitions, respectively (Chen et al., 2010; Mahalingam et al., 2009). Notably, the intensity of NIR-to NIR emission at around 796nm is much stronger than other peaks, suggesting that the NIR emission is the main radiative deactivation route for UCNPs. Therefore, the emission at 796nm was selected as quantitative signal in the following experiment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence responses towards Cu2+, PPi and ALP
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. S6 displays the time dependence of fluorescence intensity of UCNPs at 796nm in the presence of Cu2+. The fluorescence intensity remains stable after 5min incubation. Thus, 5min was chosen as the optimal incubation time for Cu2+ determination. With the addition of Cu2+, the emission intensity decreases continuously, indicating the UCNPs-based probe is very sensitive to Cu2+(Fig. S7 and Fig. 1A). Fig. 1B shows the plot of fluorescence quenching efficiency against the different concentration of Cu2+, which exhibits a good linear ranging from 0.1 to 2.0μM. The limit of detection (LOD=3σ/k, where σ is the standard variance of the blank determination, and k is the slope of the linear regression equation, respectively) of Cu2+ was calculated to be 57.8nM. At higher concentration of Cu2+, the fluorescence quenching efficiency reaches a plateau, indicating the saturation of the chelating sites after Cu2+ binding. Thus, 2.0μM Cu2+ was chosen for the following quantitative determination of PPi and ALP. To further prove the specificity of the developed sensor for Cu2+, the effects of other cations on the fluorescent signal were investigated, including Ca2+, Co2+, Fe3+, K+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Na+, Pb2+, Hg2+, NH4 +, Fe2+, and Zn2+. The concentration of Cu2+ was maintained at 2.0μM and the concentrations of other cations were 20.0μM. As shown in Fig. 1C, no apparent signal change is observed for other cations in contrast with Cu2+. Compared with some references (Table S1), the nanoprobe has the advantages of facile synthesis, detection in aqueous media, high sensitivity and selectivity.

Previous studies reported that amine groups can coordinate selectively with Cu2+(Dong et al., 2012; Gao et al., 2016; Zong et al., 2011). As shown in Fig. S8A, the appearance of a small shoulder at ~280nm in UV–Vis spectrum preliminarily implies the formation of cupric amine coordinates based on the interaction between UCNPs and Cu2+. It is also noted that the chelation of Cu2+ with PEI results in an obvious absorption band at 275nm and a broad absorption ranging from 500 to 900nm (Fig. S8B), which is consistent with previous reports (Dong et al., 2012; Yuan et al., 2014). The broad absorption band overlaps with emission peaks of the UCNPs (650, 702 and 796nm) (Fig. S8B). However, the intensity of the broad absorption (in the presence of 300μM Cu2+) is very weak (molar absorption coefficient of cupric amine complex is calculated as 110Lmol−1 cm−1), and the emission peaks at 451 and 478nm are not overlapped. Therefore, inner-filter effect is not the dominant mechanism for the fluorescence quenching. Inspired by previous studies (Aidilibike et al., 2016; Sarkar et al., 2015, 2014), we speculate that energy transfer might be the main reason for the Cu2+-induced fluorescence quenching of UCNPs. The energy level diagram and plausible energy transfer mechanism from Yb3+ to Cu2+ is shown in Fig. 1D, in which the energy levels of Cu2+ are referred based on the literature (Sarkar et al., 2014). The presence of Cu2+ on the surface of nanocrystals may disturb the energy transfer from Yb3+ to Tm3+. Research revealed the possibility of Yb3+-assisted upconversion process in the NaYF4 under excitation of 980nm, and the Yb3+-trimer luminescence peak at ~343nm (Qin et al., 2014) has an overlap with the absorption spectrum of cupric amine coordinates (blue line in Fig. S8B). Upon 980nm excitation, Yb3+ ions are excited to the 2F5/2 state. The cooperative energy transfer from Yb3+-trimer is transferred to the 1D2 level of the Cu2+. The de-excitation of the Cu2+ to the ground state occurs nonradiatively (Bünzli and Piguet, 2002). To further verify our proposed energy transfer quenching mechanism, time-resolved fluorescence spectra of the PEI-capped UCNPs were obtained before and after addition of Cu2+, respectively (Fig. S9). It can be seen that the fluorescence lifetime for PEI-capped UCNPs at 796nm is 156.15μs. The lifetime is decayed to 147.46μs in the presence of 10μM Cu2+. With the increase of concentration of Cu2+(50μM), the lifetime is further reduced (140.39μs). The results further support the energy transfer mechanism in the PEI-capped UCNPs based sensing system.

We further evaluated the feasibility of UCNPs-based fluorescent sensing platform for PPi and ALP detection with the assistance of Cu2+(Fig. S10). As shown in Fig. S11, the binding time between PPi and Cu2+ was optimized to be 5min. Different concentrations of PPi were added over a range from 0.5 to 10.0μM in the presence of 2.0μM Cu2+. As shown in Fig. 2A, the fluorescence intensity of UCNPs is increased with the addition of PPi. Fig. 2B shows the plot of (F/F0−1) against the concentrations of PPi, and a good linear response is obtained over the range from 0.5 to 8.0μM with the LOD of 184nM (F/F0−1=0.2045 [PPi]+0.1408). As shown in Table S1, the LOD towards PPi is lower or comparable to these methods based on Ag nanoclusters (Ma et al., 2016), carbon quantum dots (Qian et al., 2015), and Au nanoclusters (Chen et al., 2014). To validate the specificity of the interaction between Cu2+ and PPi, the effects of PPi and other potential interferences on the fluorescent response were investigated, including ATP, PO4 3－, HPO4 2－, H2PO4 －, HCO3 －, CO3 2－, SO4 2-, and F－. As shown in Fig. 2C, the specificity of probe for PPi is very well. The results also demonstrate that the possible hydrolysis products of PPi catalyzed by ALP, such as PO4 3－, HPO4 2－, and H2PO4 －, do not affect the competitive assay. Thus, the ALP activity assay is feasible based on the “switch-off” fluorescence of UCNPs.

It is well-known that ALP plays an important role in clinical practice, and the quantitative detection is of great significance. The linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity of UCNPs and the concentrations of PPi is of benefit to the subsequent quantitative ALP activity assay. Fig. 3A shows that the intensity of UCNPs gradually decreases with the increase of ALP in the presence of 8.0μM PPi. ALP-directed PPi hydrolysis leads to the disassembly of Cu-PPi complex. In addition, the released Cu2+ ions are reconjugated with PEI and induce the fluorescence quenching of UCNPs. As shown in Fig. 3B, the fluorescence quenching efficiency increases linearly with ALP concentration over the range of 0.0625–0.875U/mL. The LOD of ALP is calculated to be 0.019U/mL, which is comparable to that of polymer NPs-based method (Deng et al., 2015) (shown in Table S1). To validate the specificity for ALP detection, different analytes were investigated, including ALP, GOD, EXO I, lysozyme, BSA, and trypsin. It can be seen from Fig. 3C that none of them can quench the fluorescence except for ALP. Thus, the nanoprobe is very selective for ALP determination.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of PPi in human urine
ce:section.ce:para: To demonstrate the potential application of our method in real samples, PPi was detected in human urine from healthy adults. The 40-fold dilution urine samples were added into the sensing system and the corresponding fluorescence intensities were recorded. Taking the dilution into account, the initial concentration of PPi in human urine was determined to be 51.64±2.72, 40.48±2.49, and 61.50±3.03μM, respectively. The results were consistent with the PPi concentration in human urine obtained by Shao (Shao et al., 2010). As shown in Table S2, the recoveries for PPi measurement are in the range of 96.05–106.07% with desirable RSD. These results indicate that the nanoprobe is reliable and practical for PPi detection in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of ALP in serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to test whether the fluorescent sensing system could be further used in more complex samples, the detection of ALP spiked in 1% diluted bovine serum were performed using Cu2+-PEI-UCNPs based probe. The complex components in bovine serum may influence the working concentration of Cu2+. Thus, it is necessary to optimize the Cu2+ concentration for real sample analysis. As shown in Fig. S12, the quenching ability of Cu2+ towards fluorescent UCNPs is retarded in the diluted bovine serum with respect to that in HEPES buffer, however, it could be compensated by adding more Cu2+ into the system. It can be seen that the best performance can be achieved at 15.0μM Cu2+. Using the optimized Cu2+ concentration, PPi with different concentration in bovine serum can be detected (Fig. S13). For the precise detection of ALP in real samples, the fluorescent response of the biosensing system was re-evaluated in 1% diluted bovine serum ( Fig. 4A). The linear equation for ALP detection is (1-F/F0) =0.4911[ALP]−0.01670 in the presence of 10.0μM PPi (Fig. 4B). The quantitative detection of ALP was achieved with an acceptable recovery of 93.70–105.27% and relative standard deviations (RSD) of 1.55–3.97% (Table S3). More importantly, the performance on fluorescence recovery by PPi and the following fluorescence decrease induced by ALP in diluted bovine serum is comparable to that in HEPES buffer, indicating that the assay has potential to apply in biological samples.

To further demonstrate the potential application of our method in real serum samples, the fluorescence probe was applied for the determination of ALP in human serum from healthy adults. As for ALP detection, the values in human serum were in good agreement with ELISA method which required tedious procedures (Table S4). These results indicated that the probe is reliable and practical for ALP detection in human serums.

To evaluate the potential application of PEI-capped UCNPs in living cells, the effect of UCNPs on proliferation of HeLa cells was investigated by a standard MTT assay. The HeLa cells were incubated with PEI-capped UCNPs with concentration ranging from 0 to 200μg/mL for 24h. As shown in Fig. S14, the cell viability is very well even the concentration of the nanoprobe is up to 200μg/mL. The results show that the cytotoxicity of PEI-capped UCNPs is very weak, and the probes can be applied in bioimaging.

Owing to the well biocompatibility and NIR feature of the UCNPs, the fluorescence probe for intracellular Cu2+ imaging was further investigated. As shown in Fig. 5A, an intense fluorescence signal from UCNPs can be observed under the excitation at 980nm by a CLSUCLM, indicating the efficient uptake of the nanoprobe and the ability of bioimaging in living cells. Furthermore, as depicted in Fig. 5B, the fluorescence signal is reduced with the treatment of exogenous Cu2+ source (140μM) for 15min. While the Hela cells are further incubated with additional 80μM Cu2+ for another 10min, the intracellular fluorescence signal is significantly quenched (Fig. 5C). These results successfully demonstrate that Cu2+-induced fluorescence quenching of UCNPs internalized by Hela cells could be used for effective bioimaging of intracellular Cu2+.

In summary, a novel and label-free fluorescence assay was developed for sequential detection of Cu2+, PPi and ALP using branched PEI-capped UCNPs-based probes. The as-constructed upconversion fluorescence biosensor possesses high sensitivity, good stability and selectivity. It was successfully applied in the detection of PPi and ALP in human urine and serum samples, respectively. In addition, taking the advantages of NIR imaging as well as the excellent biocompatibility of the UCNPs, the nanoprobe was also used for the fluorescence imaging of Cu2+ in living cells with satisfactory results.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21605050), Shanghai Natural Science Foundation (15ZR1411600) and the Postdoctoral Science Foundation of China (2015M570349).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.bios.2017.04.010.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Table S1
Supplementary material
.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: Valentine, J.S., Hart, P.J., 2003. Proceedings Nat. Acad. Sci. U. S. A. 100, 3617–3622.
